focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The ledger stuck to Silas's forearm when he turned the page, then gave with the little kiss of paper peeling off itself. Noon heat had packed the propagation shed into a wet lung. Pipe warmth breathed through the plank walls, burlap sweated on the tray racks, and the oilskin cover had gone tacky with old lamp grease. He had never seen a book that felt so much like a tool. Its corners were blunted by years of wet hands, and one scorched patch near the spine showed where somebody had set it too close to a burner.

Inez weighed the ledger flat with two stone sinkers and bent over it in her work shirt, sleeves rolled, hair wrapped up and damp at the nape. The burn scar inside her right wrist shone pearly when the shed light hit it. She had been warmer with him before dawn, warmer and lazier and willing to laugh into his throat when he said something stupid with his face in her shoulder. By daylight she had gone back to precision, sleeves rolled and ledger pinned flat. The warmth was still there; it had tightened into the kind of trust that expected him to keep up.

"Stop reading the names first," she said.

He looked up from a column that ran mudglow, widow-step, pan-blue, drain-hush. "What else did your mother put in here worth my humiliation?"

"The part that keeps people from getting burned."

He made a face, but he obeyed. The names stood at the edge of each entry, small and practical, as if embarrassed by how much men liked them. The real body of the page was measurement, heat behavior, smoke quality, where a cutting would hold steady, which houses used it, what not to do if children were in reach. Some entries had a little hooked mark in the margin. Some had three pin dots, close together like nail holes. Beside several of those, in different hands and d...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e spacing gave the work away even if the painter did not: the same measured breath around the words, the same expensive calm. And the rest of the job came with it: the teacher's copy sheet, the clerk's curled lip, the old name left to weather at the bottom edge.

His wrist ached in time with the ferry engine. He rested it on the rail and looked until the ache stopped hanging over the whole day like a signboard with his name on it and became only one hurt among others. He thought of Junie's kitchen, Otis's copy sheet, the warning board painted over, the woman rescuing a discarded plank as if it had family in it, and Inez's people hearing their rough practical speech come back from a clerk's mouth all combed and expensive.

He had thought theft was the moment a man pocketed a word that was not his. Now he could see the slower labor that made the taking stick: sanding dust in the seams, pale wash over old warnings, a schoolroom hand learning the approved loops.

When the ferry slid back against Ferrymouth and the ropes flew, he got off without hurrying. Junie's sign still hung over the kitchen door, dark letters on honest board, broth and bread promised plain. Evening damp had deepened the blue strokes he had laid there years ago. For the first time all day he did not look at it as proof of what his hand used to do.

He looked at the nail heads, the weathered edge, the room a new panel would need. He had spent years teaching walls to flatter strangers. He could already see the cleaner board arriving for this one: pale ground, calm spacing, somebody else's promise nailed over his blue, and after that over every rough useful word south of the cut. Come dark he'd have to carry his shame over the drainage cut to the west houses and ask what work was left for his one good hand.
